sections.title: üßí The Story
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Every team says "the system is down."
But until you can answer these three questions, you are still guessing:
‚Üí what is broken?
‚Üí where is it broken?
‚Üí since when?

Observability is what turns panic into diagnosis.

METRICS answer:
‚Üí is the system healthy right now?

LOGS answer:
‚Üí what exactly happened?

TRACES answer:
‚Üí where did the time go across services?

AUDIT TRAILS answer:
‚Üí who changed what?

MENTAL MODEL:
Monitoring tells you there is pain.
Observability helps you explain the pain quickly enough to fix it.
```

---

blocks.content:
Every team says "the system is down."
But until you can answer these three questions, you are still guessing:
‚Üí what is broken?
‚Üí where is it broken?
‚Üí since when?

Observability is what turns panic into diagnosis.

METRICS answer:
‚Üí is the system healthy right now?

LOGS answer:
‚Üí what exactly happened?

TRACES answer:
‚Üí where did the time go across services?

AUDIT TRAILS answer:
‚Üí who changed what?

MENTAL MODEL:
Monitoring tells you there is pain.
Observability helps you explain the pain quickly enough to fix it.

revisionPoints: ‚Üí is the system healthy right now?
‚Üí where did the time go across services?

sections.title: üìä CloudWatch ‚Äî Deep Dive
sections.rawMarkdown:
### Metrics
```
NAMESPACE: logical grouping of metrics
‚Üí AWS/EC2, AWS/Lambda, AWS/RDS, YourApp/BusinessMetrics

DIMENSIONS: attributes to filter metrics
‚Üí EC2: InstanceId=i-abc123, InstanceType=t3.medium
‚Üí Lambda: FunctionName=myFunction, Resource=myFunction:PROD

RESOLUTION:
Standard: 1-minute granularity (default for most AWS services)
High-resolution: 1-second granularity (for your custom metrics)

BUILT-IN METRICS BY SERVICE:

EC2:
CPUUtilization, NetworkIn, NetworkOut, DiskReadOps, DiskWriteOps
StatusCheckFailed (hardware or software check failed)
NOT included by default: Memory, disk space (need CloudWatch Agent)

Lambda:
Invocations, Errors, Duration, Throttles, ConcurrentExecutions
DeadLetterErrors, IteratorAge (for stream-based triggers ‚Äî staleness)

RDS/Aurora:
CPUUtilization, DatabaseConnections, FreeStorageSpace
ReadLatency, WriteLatency, FreeableMemory, ReplicaLag

ALB:
RequestCount, TargetResponseTime (latency), HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count
HealthyHostCount, UnHealthyHostCount

SQS:
ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible (queue depth)
ApproximateAgeOfOldestMessage, NumberOfMessagesSent

CUSTOM METRICS:
aws cloudwatch put-metric-data \
  --namespace "MyApp/Business" \
  --metric-name "OrdersPlaced" \
  --value 47 \
  --unit Count \
  --dimensions Environment=Production

‚Üí Track: active users, orders placed, revenue, API errors, custom business KPIs
‚Üí Cost: $0.30/metric/month (first 10,000 metrics free)
```

### CloudWatch Alarms
```
ALARM STATES:
OK: metric within threshold
ALARM: metric breached threshold
INSUFFICIENT_DATA: not enough data to determine

ACTIONS ON ALARM:
‚Üí SNS notification ‚Üí email/Slack/PagerDuty
‚Üí EC2 Auto Scaling: scale in or scale out
‚Üí EC2 action: stop, terminate, reboot, recover instance
‚Üí Systems Manager OpsItem

ALARM TYPES:
Metric Alarm: watch one metric vs threshold
Composite Alarm: combine multiple alarms with AND/OR logic
"ALARM if CPU > 80% AND Memory > 90% (both must be true)"

MUST-HAVE ALARMS FOR PRODUCTION:
EC2:      CPUUtilization > 80%, StatusCheckFailed > 0
Lambda:   Errors > 0.1%, Throttles > 0, Duration P99 > 5000ms
RDS:      CPUUtilization > 80%, DatabaseConnections > 80% of max
          FreeStorageSpace < 20% of total, ReplicaLag > 60s
ALB:      HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count > 10, TargetResponseTime P99 > 2000ms
SQS:      ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible on DLQ > 0 (something failing!)
Billing:  EstimatedCharges > your monthly budget threshold
```

### CloudWatch Logs
```
LOG GROUP: container for a service's logs (/aws/lambda/myFunction)
LOG STREAM: one log stream per instance/invocation
RETENTION: 1 day to NEVER (default: never = costly! Set retention!)

SENDING LOGS:
‚Üí Lambda: automatic (via execution role + awslogs)
‚Üí EC2: install CloudWatch Agent (needed for app logs, not just console output)
‚Üí ECS: awslogs log driver in task definition (no agent needed)
‚Üí API Gateway: enable in stage settings
‚Üí Your app: CloudWatch SDK or structured logging to stdout

CLOUDWATCH LOGS INSIGHTS (query language):
# Find all errors in last hour
fields @timestamp, @message
| filter @message like /ERROR/
| sort @timestamp desc
| limit 100

# Lambda cold start analysis
filter @type = "REPORT"
| stats avg(@initDuration), max(@initDuration) by bin(5m)

# P99 Lambda duration
filter @type = "REPORT"
| stats pct(@duration, 99) as p99Duration by bin(1h)

# Count 5xx errors per minute
filter status >= 500
| stats count(*) as error_count by bin(1m)
| sort error_count desc

METRIC FILTERS:
‚Üí Extract metrics FROM log patterns
‚Üí Example: count lines matching "ERROR" ‚Üí publish as metric ‚Üí alarm on it
‚Üí Count specific exception type ‚Üí metric ‚Üí alarm
‚Üí No need to instrument code separately if logs already contain the data

SUBSCRIPTION FILTERS:
‚Üí Stream logs in real-time to Lambda (process) or Kinesis (stream)
‚Üí Use for: real-time log analytics, sending to Elasticsearch, alerting
```

---

METRIC FILTERS:

‚Üí No need to instrument code separately if logs already contain the data

sections.title: üîç CloudTrail ‚Äî Audit Trail
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
CloudTrail records EVERY API call in your AWS account.
Who did what, when, from where, with what result.

EVENT TYPES:
Management Events (control plane):
‚Üí CreateBucket, DeleteInstance, CreateUser, AttachPolicy
‚Üí Free for first copy in CloudTrail console (Event History: 90 days)
‚Üí Additional trails: charges apply

Data Events (data plane):
‚Üí S3 GetObject, PutObject, DeleteObject (object-level)
‚Üí Lambda InvokeFunction
‚Üí DynamoDB GetItem, PutItem
‚Üí Not captured by default (very high volume, extra cost)
‚Üí Enable selectively for sensitive buckets/functions

CLOUDTRAIL LAKE:
‚Üí Managed data lake for CloudTrail events
‚Üí Query with SQL via Athena integration
‚Üí Run complex audit queries: "all API calls by user X in last 6 months"
‚Üí Retention: 7 years (for compliance)

WHAT CLOUDTRAIL CAPTURES:
{
  "userIdentity": {
    "type": "IAMUser",
    "arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456:user/alice",
    "accessKeyId": "AKIA..."
  },
  "eventTime": "2024-01-15T14:30:00Z",
  "eventSource": "s3.amazonaws.com",
  "eventName": "DeleteObject",
  "awsRegion": "us-east-1",
  "sourceIPAddress": "203.0.113.50",
  "requestParameters": {
    "bucketName": "prod-customer-data",
    "key": "users/12345/personal.pdf"
  }
}

ENABLE CLOUDTRAIL BEST PRACTICES:
‚úÖ Enable in ALL regions (attackers often use less-watched regions)
‚úÖ Enable log file validation (detects tampering with logs)
‚úÖ Store in dedicated S3 bucket (separate account ideally)
‚úÖ Enable CloudWatch Logs integration (query + alarm on specific events)
‚úÖ Retain logs: 1+ year for security, 7 years for compliance

USEFUL CLOUDTRAIL ALERTS:
‚Üí Root account login (should never happen)
‚Üí IAM policy change (unexpected permission escalation)
‚Üí CloudTrail disabled (attacker covering tracks)
‚Üí Failed console login 5+ times (brute force attempt)
‚Üí New IAM user with admin access created
‚Üí Security Group modified to allow port 22 from 0.0.0.0/0
```

---

blocks.content:
CloudTrail records EVERY API call in your AWS account.
Who did what, when, from where, with what result.

EVENT TYPES:
Management Events (control plane):

USEFUL CLOUDTRAIL ALERTS:

‚Üí Not captured by default (very high volume, extra cost)

sections.title: üî≠ X-Ray ‚Äî Distributed Tracing
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
WHAT X-RAY DOES:
‚Üí Traces requests through your entire application
‚Üí Shows: which service handled it, how long each step took, where errors occurred
‚Üí Works across: Lambda, EC2, ECS, API Gateway, SQS, DynamoDB, RDS, HTTP calls

WITHOUT X-RAY:
User reports: "my checkout is slow"
You: look at Lambda logs ‚Üí look at RDS logs ‚Üí look at external API logs...
‚Üí Manually correlate across 5 different log streams
‚Üí Takes hours to find the bottleneck

WITH X-RAY:
‚Üí Single trace shows complete request journey
‚Üí Timeline visualization: API Gateway (10ms) ‚Üí Lambda (50ms) ‚Üí RDS query (2000ms!)
‚Üí Found it: RDS query is the bottleneck ‚Üí add index ‚Üí fixed

CONCEPTS:
Trace: one end-to-end request (has unique trace ID)
Segment: one service's contribution (Lambda segment, RDS segment)
Subsegment: detailed breakdown within a segment (specific DB query)
Annotation: key-value pairs for filtering traces (userId, orderId)
Metadata: additional data (not indexed, not searchable)

SERVICE MAP:
‚Üí Visual graph of your architecture
‚Üí Shows: services, their connections, error rates, latency
‚Üí Color-coded health: green = healthy, yellow = slow, red = errors
‚Üí Click a service ‚Üí see its traces

ENABLE X-RAY:
Lambda: Enable Active Tracing in function config
         (or POWERTOOLS_TRACER_CAPTURE_RESPONSE=true env var)
ECS: Add X-Ray sidecar container to task definition
EC2: Install X-Ray daemon on instance
API Gateway: Enable X-Ray tracing on stage
SDK: Wrap SDK clients with X-Ray (automatic segment creation)

SAMPLING:
‚Üí Tracing every request = high cost (large apps = millions of requests)
‚Üí Sampling: trace a percentage of requests
‚Üí Default: 1 request/second + 5% of additional requests
‚Üí Custom rules: trace 100% of requests with errors, 1% of successful
‚Üí Cost: $5.00 per million traces recorded (first 100K/month free)
```

---

‚Üí Sampling: trace a percentage of requests
‚Üí Default: 1 request/second + 5% of additional requests

sections.title: üéØ Interview Q&A ‚Äî Observability
sections.rawMarkdown:
**Q: A service is 'slow', but CPU looks fine everywhere. How do you lead that investigation?**
```
"CPU being normal just tells me the bottleneck is elsewhere.

I would build a latency breakdown:
‚Üí ALB/API Gateway latency
‚Üí app latency
‚Üí downstream database latency
‚Üí cache hit rate
‚Üí external API latency

Then I would use:
‚Üí CloudWatch metrics for high-level symptoms
‚Üí logs for error context and request IDs
‚Üí X-Ray or tracing for end-to-end timeline

The key senior move is correlating one user request across systems instead of looking at
one dashboard at a time. Most 'slow app' incidents turn out to be a dependency,
lock contention, bad query plan, or retry storm rather than raw CPU shortage."
```

**Q: What logs, metrics, and alarms are mandatory before you call a system production-ready?**
```
"At minimum I want three layers:

Golden signals:
‚Üí latency
‚Üí traffic
‚Üí errors
‚Üí saturation

Business signals:
‚Üí orders placed
‚Üí checkout success rate
‚Üí payment failures
‚Üí queue age / backlog growth

Operational alarms:
‚Üí error budget burn or 5xx spike
‚Üí DLQ > 0
‚Üí database connections near max
‚Üí latency p95/p99 above threshold
‚Üí no traffic when there should be traffic

And I want structured logs with correlation IDs.
Senior teams do not just log errors; they make incidents searchable and explainable."
```

**Q: CloudTrail shows who changed something, but how do you turn that into a real incident response workflow?**
```
"CloudTrail alone is just evidence. The production pattern is:
CloudTrail ‚Üí CloudWatch/EventBridge rule ‚Üí SNS/PagerDuty/Lambda automation.

Examples:
‚Üí root login detected ‚Üí page security team immediately
‚Üí security group opened to 0.0.0.0/0 on SSH ‚Üí revert automatically or quarantine
‚Üí CloudTrail disabled ‚Üí critical severity incident

The mature answer is that audit logs must be connected to alerting and response,
otherwise you only discover the problem during postmortem."
```

---

sections.title: üîó Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
CloudWatch ‚Üê every AWS service: metrics and alarms
CloudWatch Logs ‚Üê Lambda/ECS/EC2/API Gateway/apps: operational detail
CloudTrail ‚Üê all AWS API calls: control plane audit
X-Ray ‚Üê API Gateway/Lambda/ECS/SDK calls: request tracing
Observability ‚Üí SNS/PagerDuty/Slack: incident alerting
Observability ‚Üí Auto Scaling/runbooks: automated response

NEXT: Security ‚Äî KMS, Secrets Manager, WAF, GuardDuty, Shield
```
